metadata.title: Claude, Derived.
metadata.description: Skill teardown of the math-explainer (Brown Blue) pipeline — the 3Blue1Brown-style pure-Manim concrete-before-abstract builder.

skin_warnings: B03: composer beat has an empty spark line — SPARK-LINE LAW wants a short serif cue (e.g. "The change,").

beats.narration_text: Hallo — this is Liam, in for Bear. The math-explainer skill is a 3Blue1Brown clone built on one sequence: mystery first, concrete always, abstraction earned as the endpoint. Nothing gets defined before the viewer feels why they need it. This episode is the skill explaining that law — the irony is intended.

output: Law: concrete before abstract, enforced and audited
Pipeline: script → beats → audio → Manim → clean master

beats.narration_text: The skill lives in skills/make/math-explainer/. SKILL.md is the instruction. Under it: reference/pedagogy.md enforces the 3b1b sequencing gates — mystery opening, concrete-before-abstract, transform-don't-cut. Reference/style.md holds the palette: EB Garamond, blue and brown on a dark canvas. Reference/equations.md governs the equation tangent that fires every time a formula lands on screen.

The pipeline has six phases. Mystery opening first — the key exercise or key case, unsolved. Then concrete instances, where the viewer meets the idea in the particular before the general. Then transformation beats: objects morph on screen, no cuts. Then abstraction — finally earned by the concrete work. Then the equation tangent fires every time a formula lands. Then the clean master ships, approved first, before any caption burn. Three gates guard the sequence.

props.title: Mystery earns abstraction.
props.footerNote: Gate 1: script → Gate 2: beats (abstraction audit) → Gate 3: audio — cheap before expensive.

beats.narration_text: Run it on a derivative concept — why is the derivative of x-squared exactly 2x? The skill scopes the one insight, classifies it as a problem type, derives the length tier, and writes a beat sheet with the 3b1b arc. The beat sheet is the output.

props.runningText: mode: B — mystery opening + concrete arc
output: * insight scoped: derivative as slope of tangent line
* type: problem — opens with unsolved key exercise
* length tier: SHORT (4–8 min) · beat sheet ready
* Gate 1 audit: mystery ✓ concrete first ✓ abstract earned ✓

beats.narration_text: The beat sheet shows the 3b1b sequence in action. The mystery beat comes first — the exercise is posed before any definition. Then a concrete instance shows one specific case. Then a transform beat morphs that instance into the next. The abstraction beat names the limit formally — only after the viewer has seen it move. The equation tangent fires immediately after, in a bracket.

props.title: beat_sheet.json — math-explainer output

beats.narration_text: The concrete-before-abstract law is the signature constraint. No general definition, no new notation, until the viewer has a felt reason to want it. The beat sheet is rejected at Gate 2 if any abstraction beat precedes its concrete instances. This is audited by the skill — not aspirational. A beat sheet that skips the concrete is a Gate-2 rejection.

props.heading: The definition waits.
props.body: No general definition, no new notation, until the viewer has a felt reason to want it. The beat sheet is rejected at Gate 2 if any abstraction beat precedes its concrete instances. The skill enforces the sequence — not as a style preference, but as a gate.
props.quote: Concrete before abstract — enforced, not aspirational.

beats.narration_text: The transform-don't-cut law governs animation. Within a scene, state changes are animated morphs of persistent objects — Transform, animate-apply-matrix, ReplacementTransform. The algebra formalizes what was just seen moving. A cut between static states is a law violation. CUT beats are allowed only at act boundaries.

props.heading: The algebra earns its name.
props.body: Within a scene, state changes are animated morphs — Transform, animate.apply_matrix, ReplacementTransform. Objects persist; they do not disappear and reappear. The viewer sees the algebra follow from the geometry, because the geometry moved first. A static cut breaks the causal thread.
props.quote: transform, don't cut — objects morph; the algebra formalizes what was just seen moving

beats.narration_text: The design tell is the equation tangent. Every time a finished equation lands on screen, a forty-five-second bracket fires — five zones that explain the equation without deriving it. This is not skippable. A landed equation with no tangent is a Gate-2 rejection. The skill trusts explanation over proof, and the tangent is where that trust is made structural.

props.heading: The equation gets its 45 seconds.
props.body: Every landed equation fires the tangent bracket — five zones: LHS/RHS claim, symbol glossary, worked example, commitment, re-entry. The bracket explains, never derives. A landed equation with no tangent bracket is a Gate-2 rejection. The tangent is not optional: it is where the skill makes explanation structural.
props.quote: Every equation fires the equation tangent.

beats.narration_text: Claude, Derived. What the skill actually does. One: mystery opening — the key exercise unsolved, before any definition. Two: concrete before abstract, enforced and audited at Gate 2. Three: transform, don't cut — objects morph across the arc, never disappear and reappear. Four: equation tangent — every landed formula earns a 45-second explanation bracket. The abstraction is the destination, not the starting point.

artifactLines: Mystery opening: key exercise unsolved, before any definition.
Concrete before abstract: enforced and audited at Gate 2 — a beat sheet that skips the instances is rejected.
Transform, don't cut: objects morph; the algebra formalizes what was just seen moving.
Equation tangent: every landed formula fires a 45-second explanation bracket — not optional.

beats.narration_text: Your turn. Use the math-explainer skill on a concept you can state as a question that starts with 'why does' or 'what makes'. Start with: what concrete instance would make a viewer feel why the abstraction is needed? Paste into Claude: dot slash art math-explainer, then your question.

output: Use the math-explainer skill on a concept you can
state as a question: 'why does...' or 'what makes...'
Start with: what concrete instance makes a viewer feel
